content.problemStatement:
Chef is playing with numbers and came across an interesting problem. Given two positive integers nnn and mmm, Chef wants to find an integer XXX such that when XXX is raised to the power of NNN, it equals MMM.

Formally, find an integer XXX satisfying:

Xn=mX^n = mXn=m

If no such integer exists, output −1-1−1.

Can you help Chef find the answer?

Function DeclarationFunction NamenthRootnthRootnthRoot – This function finds the integer NthN^\text{th}Nth root of a number if it exists.

Parameters
nnn : A positive integer representing the power.
mmm : A positive integer representing the target value.
Return Value
Returns an integer XXX such that Xn=mX^n = mXn=m.
Returns −1-1−1 if no such integer exists.
Constraints
1≤T≤10001 \leq T ≤ 10001≤T≤1000
1≤n≤301 \leq n \leq 301≤n≤30
1≤m≤1091 \leq m \leq 10^91≤m≤109
XXX must be an integer

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each of the next TTT lines contains two space-separated integers nnn and mmm.


Output Format

For each test case, print a single integer — the value of XXX if it exists, otherwise −1-1−1.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated positive integers n and m.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer X such that Xⁿ = m. If no integer X satisfies the equation, output -1. Each answer is printed on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: 2‑th root of 16 is 4 because 4²=16.
Test case 2: 5‑th root of 243 is 3 because 3⁵=243.
Test case 3: No integer X satisfies X³=10, so output -1.
Test case 4: 6‑th root of 729 is 3 because 3⁶=729.

solution.approach: For each test case we need the integer n‑th root of m, if it exists. The function f(x)=xⁿ is strictly increasing for x≥1, so we can binary‑search the answer in the range [1, m]. During the search we compute midⁿ but stop as soon as the product exceeds m to avoid overflow. If the computed power equals m we have found the root; otherwise after the loop no integer satisfies the equation and we output -1. The per‑test‑case time is O(log m·n) and the memory usage is O(1).
solution.timeComplexity: O(T·n·log m) – each power evaluation costs O(n) and binary search performs O(log m) iterations.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
